6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Lately, I have been thinking deeply about memory. How it works in us, how we use it, and what it might teach us about building memory for AI. These were bedtime thoughts, the kind that arrive uninvited and refuse to leave. It was existential in a quieter way. An acknowledgment of what we are made of, and how little of it we actually keep.
A whole year can disappear
Here is a strange exercise. Pick a year of school. Any year. For some of them, you might have almost zero recall. Months of your life, hundreds of days, and nothing surfaces. Then think of a year when something memorable happened, even something remotely memorable, and suddenly everything comes back with it. The room. The seating arrangement. Who sat where? What happened before and after? One event pins an entire context in place, and the mundane days around it get carried along for free.
That pinning seems to be how the whole system works. Rare and countable things mostly stick. The houses you have lived in. All the different colors. The flights you have taken. The goals you have scored. The cities you have visited. The movies you have watched, at least the plots, even ones you saw years ago. These live in neat compartments, and the compartments have adaptive context. Sometimes the context of a memory is a few seconds. Sometimes…
